caveats: a: No public six-digit occupation mix was located.
a: Current automation penetration in CAD/CAM, CNC nesting, and estimating is not measured, so already-automated work may be overstated.
rho: No longitudinal AI implementation study for US prefab wood manufacturers was located.
rho: Results will differ between standardized panels and bespoke volumetric projects.
e: The injected n is margin-bridged from size-class averages and may not correspond to standalone transferable firms.
e: The NAICS spans components, complete panelized units, precut packages, and volumetric buildings with different recurrence patterns.
s5: Cross-industry intentions are an imperfect proxy for completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Strategic asset purchases, internal family transfers, and distressed plant sales may not preserve a standalone eligible firm.
q: Public sources do not measure productivity pass-through.
q: The mix of fixed-price, cost-plus, component-supply, and design-build contracts varies widely.
d5: Residential completion shares do not cover all commercial and component demand.
d5: Demand is cyclical and exposed to interest rates, construction finance, building codes, local approvals, transport radius, and conventional-building competition.
o: Operator requirement is distinct from labor intensity.
o: Some design and configuration services may be self-served even though physical production remains external.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is principally project, module, panel, component, or complete-building sales under bids and supply contracts; repeat business can come from builders and developers, but it is not usually subscription or recurring-service revenue.
lens.deviation_from_default: This is product and project manufacturing, not a recurring outsourced-service industry. The lens therefore retains only firms with repeat external B2B production relationships, a coherence-based narrowing.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufactured mobile homes, conventional onsite general contractors without manufacturing, captive fabrication units, project shells, one-off hobby structures, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent US manufacturers of prefabricated stationary wood buildings, volumetric modules, panelized systems, and precut building packages that serve repeat builders, developers, public agencies, or commercial customers and have transferable standalone operations.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover the broader wood-product subsector/NAICS 321900 rather than 321992. I assign exposure to estimating, BIM/design documentation, procurement, scheduling, compliance, sales, and administrative tasks, and little direct language-model exposure to assembly and machine operation. Prefabricated-building firms may contain more design/project coordination than general wood products, so the broader mix could understate exposure; it may also contain less standardized factory work, creating offsetting error.
caveats: No public six-digit occupation mix was located.
Current automation penetration in CAD/CAM, CNC nesting, and estimating is not measured, so already-automated work may be overstated.
a.change_evidence: Wage-weighted task diaries from modular, panelized, and precut-package plants, mapped to current ERP/BIM automation and O*NET tasks, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Wood-product employment is heavily physical: BLS lists 75,000 team assemblers, 38,360 woodworking-machine operators, and 30,140 sawing-machine operators in 2025. In the broader NAICS 321900 data, production alone was 51.76% of employment. Prefabricated-building work adds document-heavy design-for-manufacture, takeoff, configuration, scheduling, and project coordination, producing a moderate but clearly minority wage-weighted opportunity.

d5.bridge: NAHB/Census completion counts measure offsite construction share rather than constant-quality demand for NAICS 321992 output, and BLS projects broader NAICS 321900 real output. I use the flat 3% 2023-24 residential share as a conservative current baseline and the broader 1.7% annual output trend plus housing-productivity pressure to center modest growth. The broader category could bias either direction and offsite interest may not convert to orders.
caveats: Residential completion shares do not cover all commercial and component demand.
Demand is cyclical and exposed to interest rates, construction finance, building codes, local approvals, transport radius, and conventional-building competition.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year pipeline of contracted modules and panels, regional permit data linked to construction method, factory capacity utilization, cancellations, and constant-specification selling prices would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Only 28,000 of 1.019 million single-family completions used modular or panelized/precut methods in 2024, a 3% share unchanged from 2023 and below the 7% share in 1998. That history argues against assuming rapid adoption. Yet HUD identifies production-efficiency, quality, and cost potential, and BLS projects broader other wood-product real output growth, supporting a modest-growth base with a wide range.

caveats: The injected n is margin-bridged from size-class averages and may not correspond to standalone transferable firms.
The NAICS spans components, complete panelized units, precut packages, and volumetric buildings with different recurrence patterns.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census classifying customer concentration, repeat-customer revenue, ownership, product system, normalized EBITDA, and captive status would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Repeat supply to builders, developers, agencies, and commercial buyers creates some lens-eligible firms, but much of the industry sells one-off buildings or project-specific packages rather than a recurring outsourced service. Eligibility also requires independent control, operational continuity, and the injected EBITDA band, which public counts do not reveal.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321992 ($1,077,169,809) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321992 ($7,885,888,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $333,752 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321992); class <5: 259 firms, avg revenue/firm $799,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $117,181 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 111 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,157,117 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $316,018 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 109 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,511,037 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $660,867 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,011,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,906,257 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,560,725 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,260,646 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $200,218,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,331,971 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 160

caveats: Operator requirement is distinct from labor intensity.
Some design and configuration services may be self-served even though physical production remains external.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on builder-owned factory capacity, fully automated fabrication networks, third-party platform penetration, and customer insourcing plans would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: The current basket comprises physical engineered modules, panels, precut packages, and complete stationary buildings. Software can enable self-service configuration and automate documentation, but fabrication, code compliance, logistics, quality accountability, and warranties still require an operator. Downside reflects potential vertical integration by large builders and substitution among offsite systems.

caveats: Public sources do not measure productivity pass-through.
The mix of fixed-price, cost-plus, component-supply, and design-build contracts varies widely.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples, bid tabs, change-order histories, gross-margin bridges by project type, and interviews with repeat developers and general contractors would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Quoted project prices can protect savings on work already contracted, and improved schedule reliability may create differentiated value. Over five years, however, competitive bidding, developer purchasing sophistication, open-book or negotiated contracts, and repeat-customer repricing should share much of the gross benefit. Benefits that merely offset change orders, rework, or coordination failures may never appear as retained margin.

caveats: No longitudinal AI implementation study for US prefab wood manufacturers was located.
Results will differ between standardized panels and bespoke volumetric projects.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-project pilots reporting accepted estimate accuracy, engineering review hours, schedule adherence, change orders, rework, and warranty outcomes would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI-supported estimating, code and specification review, document generation, procurement, production planning, and project communication are implementable within five years. Realization is limited by BIM/ERP integration, site-specific designs, fragmented codes and approvals, liability, shop-floor data quality, and the need for licensed or accountable human review. HUD identifies regulation, standards, finance, project delivery, workforce, and business models as unresolved offsite-construction domains.

s5.bridge: Gallup observes five-year sale or transfer plans among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers for prefab manufacturers. I use its 22% intention rate as the anchor and reduce the base slightly because project backlog, bonding, plant assets, code approvals, and customer concentration can prevent planned transfers from qualifying or closing. Plans likely overstate completions.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are an imperfect proxy for completed qualifying transfers.
Strategic asset purchases, internal family transfers, and distressed plant sales may not preserve a standalone eligible firm.
s5.change_evidence: Ownership-age data and five years of closed prefab/modular manufacturer transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, SBA lending records, and industry brokers would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years. That supports meaningful succession pressure but not an industry-specific completion rate.

principal_driver: Digitally intensive design-to-production coordination offers implementable AI gains while the underlying physical building demand still requires an accountable operator.
principal_weakness: A small, historically flat offsite share and project-based product revenue weaken both demand visibility and fit with the recurring outsourced-service lens.
